meta.title: Strings
meta.intro: You warmed these up on paper in the String Builder and Build the Badge exercises — joining pieces with concatenation and taking substrings by position. Now type and run them for real, hardest last. Predict each output first, then check it: the first builds a badge from two substrings, and the last is an off-by-one index bug for you to fix.

problems.prompt: Given name = "Computer", print the first 4 characters followed by the last 4 characters, separated by a space.
problems.promptHtml: Given name = "Computer", print the first 4 characters followed by the last 4 characters, separated by a space.

starter.python:
name = "Computer"
# print the first 4 characters, a space, then the last 4
starter.javascript:
let name = "Computer";
// print the first 4 characters, a space, then the last 4

reference.pseudocode:
first ← first 4 characters of name
last ← last 4 characters of name
DISPLAY(first + " " + last)
(the exam describes substrings in words — there is no SUBSTRING procedure on the reference sheet)

problems.hint: name[0:4] = 'Comp', name[4:8] = 'uter'. Concatenate them with a space in the middle.

problems.prompt: Build the string "AP: 5" from course = "AP" and score = 5.
problems.hint: In Python, str(score) converts the integer to a string before you can join it with +.

problems.prompt: Print the number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u) in "programming".
problems.promptHtml: Print the number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u) in "programming".

starter.python:
word = "programming"
# count and print how many vowels it has
starter.javascript:
let word = "programming";
// count and print how many vowels it has

problems.hint: Loop over each character; check whether "aeiou" contains it, and add 1 when it does.

problems.prompt: Spot the error: this should print the 3rd character of "science" (which is 'i') but prints the wrong one. Fix it.
problems.promptHtml: Spot the error: this should print the 3rd character of "science" (which is i) but prints the wrong one. Fix it.

starter.python:
s = "science"
print(s[3])   # BUG: prints the wrong character. Fix it.
starter.javascript:
let s = "science";
console.log(s[3]);   // BUG: prints the wrong character. Fix it.

reference.pseudocode:
DISPLAY(s[3])
(exam lists are 1-indexed, so the 3rd item is index 3)

problems.hint: In Python and JavaScript, string positions start at 0 — so the 3rd character is index 2, not 3.
